title: Discussing Travel Plans
summary: Mei and Dan are planning an upcoming trip together. They discuss destination choices, budgeting, travel preferences, and how to balance their different expectations for the holiday.

body.speaker: Mei
body.text: Right, so we finally need to decide where we're going this summer. I feel like we've been talking about it for months without actually settling on anything.

body.speaker: Dan
body.text: I know, I know. The problem is we always come up with too many options. What are you actually in the mood for this time?

body.speaker: Mei
body.text: Honestly, something a bit different. I'd love to go somewhere neither of us has been before. Japan's been on my list for ages, but I know the flights aren't cheap.

body.speaker: Dan
body.text: Japan would be amazing, but I checked last week and return flights from Sydney are sitting around two thousand dollars each right now. That's before hotels and food.

body.speaker: Mei
body.text: Yeah, that might be a stretch this year. What about somewhere in Southeast Asia? Bali or Vietnam? The flights are much shorter and the budget goes a lot further.

body.speaker: Dan
body.text: Vietnam actually sounds really appealing to me. I've heard incredible things about Hoi An — the food, the old town, the cycling. It seems like the sort of place you could spend a week and still not see everything.

body.speaker: Mei
body.text: I like that idea. Though I'll be honest — I'd want to include at least a few days somewhere quieter, maybe a coastal area. I don't want the whole trip to be city sightseeing.

body.speaker: Dan
body.text: That's totally fair. We could do Hanoi and Hoi An for culture and food, then head down to Da Nang or Phu Quoc for some beach time. It gives the trip a natural rhythm.

body.speaker: Mei
body.text: Oh, that actually sounds like a really good structure. How long were you thinking? Two weeks?

body.speaker: Dan
body.text: I was going to suggest ten days to two weeks. I don't want to rush it, but I also don't want to blow through all my annual leave in one go. What have you got available?

body.speaker: Mei
body.text: I've got two weeks left, so that's fine. The tricky bit for me is keeping the overall budget manageable. I'd rather stay in mid-range guesthouses and spend more on experiences — good food, day trips, that kind of thing.

body.speaker: Dan
body.text: Completely agree. I'm not bothered about staying somewhere fancy. As long as the place is clean and comfortable, I'm happy. In Southeast Asia you can get really good value without spending a fortune.

body.speaker: Mei
body.text: Exactly. So shall we say Vietnam, roughly two weeks, mix of cities and coast? I can start looking at flights this weekend if you want to check the accommodation side.

body.speaker: Dan
body.text: Perfect. Let's set a deadline — say, end of next week — to have the flights and first few nights booked. Otherwise it'll just keep drifting.

body.speaker: Mei
body.text: Agreed. I'm actually really excited now. It feels like we've finally made a decision.

body.speaker: Dan
body.text: Only took us three months! But yes — Vietnam it is. I'll start reading up on the best spots in Hoi An tonight.

annotations.meaningVi: quyết định chọn, đưa ra quyết định cuối cùng
annotations.exampleEn: After a long debate, we finally settled on a name for the project.

annotations.meaningVi: có tâm trạng muốn, cảm thấy thích
annotations.exampleEn: I'm not really in the mood for cooking tonight — let's get takeaway.

annotations.meaningVi: trong danh sách mong muốn từ lâu
annotations.exampleEn: Visiting Peru has been on my list for ages.

annotations.meaningVi: đang ở mức khoảng, dao động quanh mức
annotations.exampleEn: Temperatures are sitting around thirty degrees this week.

annotations.meaningVi: điều khó khả thi, hơi quá sức
annotations.exampleEn: Finishing the report by Friday is a bit of a stretch, but I'll try.

annotations.phrase: the budget goes a lot further
annotations.meaningVi: tiền có thể dùng được nhiều hơn, giá cả rẻ hơn
annotations.exampleEn: In rural areas, the budget goes a lot further than in big cities.

annotations.meaningVi: rất hấp dẫn, rất thu hút
annotations.exampleEn: The idea of a weekend by the sea sounds really appealing right now.
We spent the afternoon wandering around the old town.

annotations.meaningVi: nhịp điệu tự nhiên, sự cân bằng tự nhiên
annotations.exampleEn: The trip had a natural rhythm — busy days followed by quieter ones.

annotations.phrase: blow through all my annual leave
annotations.meaningVi: dùng hết toàn bộ phép năm trong một lần
annotations.exampleEn: I don't want to blow through all my annual leave on one holiday.

annotations.meaningVi: hạng trung, không sang trọng không quá rẻ
annotations.exampleEn: We booked a mid-range hotel — not luxury, but comfortable and well-located.
You can eat well in Vietnam without spending a fortune.
Let's set a deadline for submitting the proposal.

annotations.meaningVi: tiếp tục trì hoãn, không tiến triển
annotations.exampleEn: If we don't commit to a plan, the project will just keep drifting.

annotations.meaningVi: tìm hiểu kỹ về, nghiên cứu trước về
annotations.exampleEn: She spent the evening reading up on the local history before the tour.

annotations.meaningVi: không quan tâm đến, không cần thiết
annotations.exampleEn: I'm not bothered about the décor — as long as the food is good.

grammarNotes.title: Conditional Structures — Making Plans and Suggestions
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Trong cuộc hội thoại này, cả hai người dùng nhiều cấu trúc điều kiện và câu giả định để đề xuất ý tưởng và thương lượng. 'I'd love to go somewhere' và 'I'd want to include at least a few days' dùng 'would' để diễn đạt mong muốn nhẹ nhàng, không áp đặt. 'We could do Hanoi and Hoi An' dùng 'could' để đưa ra đề nghị mang tính gợi ý. Ở B1, học viên nên luyện tập phân biệt 'would' (ý muốn, giả định) và 'could' (khả năng, đề xuất).
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Both speakers use conditional and hypothetical structures to suggest ideas and negotiate. 'I'd love to go' and 'I'd want to include' use 'would' to express preferences gently. 'We could do Hanoi and Hoi An' uses 'could' to propose an option without forcing it. B1 learners should practise distinguishing between 'would' (expressing wishes or hypotheticals) and 'could' (expressing possibility or making polite suggestions).

grammarNotes.title: Future Arrangements — Present Continuous and Going To
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Khi hai người bắt đầu lên kế hoạch cụ thể, ngôn ngữ chuyển sang thì hiện tại tiếp diễn và 'going to' để nói về những điều đã được quyết định hoặc sắp xảy ra. 'I can start looking at flights this weekend' dùng thì hiện tại tiếp diễn để nói về kế hoạch đã lên. 'I'll start reading up on the best spots' dùng 'will' cho quyết định tức thì. Học viên B1 cần nhận ra sự khác biệt giữa kế hoạch đã xác định trước và quyết định ngay tại thời điểm nói.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: As the pair commit to a plan, the language shifts to present continuous and 'will' for real arrangements. 'I can start looking at flights this weekend' uses present continuous for a scheduled plan. 'I'll start reading up tonight' uses 'will' for a spontaneous decision made in the moment. B1 learners should notice how the tense reflects whether an action was pre-planned or decided on the spot.

translationVi:
Mei: Được rồi, vậy cuối cùng chúng ta cần quyết định mùa hè này đi đâu. Tôi cảm thấy như chúng ta đã nói về điều này hàng tháng mà không thực sự quyết định được gì.
Dan: Tôi biết, tôi biết. Vấn đề là chúng ta luôn nghĩ ra quá nhiều lựa chọn. Lần này bạn thực sự muốn đi đâu?
Mei: Thành thật mà nói, tôi muốn thứ gì đó khác một chút. Tôi rất muốn đến nơi mà cả hai chưa từng đến. Nhật Bản đã nằm trong danh sách của tôi từ lâu, nhưng tôi biết vé máy bay không rẻ.
Dan: Nhật Bản thì tuyệt vời, nhưng tôi kiểm tra tuần trước và vé khứ hồi từ Sydney đang ở mức khoảng hai nghìn đô la mỗi người. Đó là chưa tính khách sạn và ăn uống.
Mei: Ừ, điều đó hơi quá sức năm nay. Còn đâu đó ở Đông Nam Á thì sao? Bali hay Việt Nam? Chuyến bay ngắn hơn nhiều và tiền tiêu được nhiều hơn.
Dan: Thực ra Việt Nam nghe rất hấp dẫn với tôi. Tôi đã nghe những điều tuyệt vời về Hội An — đồ ăn, phố cổ, đạp xe. Có vẻ như kiểu nơi mà bạn có thể ở một tuần mà vẫn chưa thấy hết mọi thứ.
Mei: Tôi thích ý tưởng đó. Mặc dù thành thật mà nói — tôi muốn bao gồm ít nhất vài ngày ở đâu đó yên tĩnh hơn, có thể là vùng ven biển. Tôi không muốn cả chuyến đi chỉ là tham quan thành phố.
Dan: Hoàn toàn hợp lý. Chúng ta có thể đến Hà Nội và Hội An để tìm hiểu văn hóa và ẩm thực, rồi di chuyển xuống Đà Nẵng hoặc Phú Quốc để nghỉ ngơi ở bãi biển. Chuyến đi sẽ có nhịp điệu tự nhiên.
Mei: Ồ, điều đó thực sự nghe như một cấu trúc tốt. Bạn nghĩ đi bao lâu? Hai tuần?
Dan: Tôi định đề nghị mười ngày đến hai tuần. Tôi không muốn vội vàng, nhưng tôi cũng không muốn dùng hết toàn bộ phép năm trong một lần. Bạn còn bao nhiêu ngày phép?
Mei: Tôi còn hai tuần, vậy không sao. Vấn đề khó với tôi là giữ tổng ngân sách ở mức hợp lý. Tôi thà ở nhà nghỉ hạng trung và chi nhiều hơn cho các trải nghiệm — đồ ăn ngon, chuyến đi trong ngày, những thứ như vậy.
Dan: Hoàn toàn đồng ý. Tôi không quan tâm đến việc ở nơi sang trọng. Miễn là chỗ ở sạch sẽ và thoải mái là được. Ở Đông Nam Á bạn có thể có giá trị thực sự tốt mà không cần tiêu tốn quá nhiều tiền.
Mei: Đúng vậy. Vậy chúng ta nói Việt Nam, khoảng hai tuần, kết hợp thành phố và bờ biển nhé? Tôi có thể bắt đầu tìm vé máy bay vào cuối tuần này nếu bạn muốn kiểm tra phần chỗ ở.
Dan: Hoàn hảo. Hãy đặt một hạn chót — giả sử, cuối tuần tới — để đặt vé máy bay và vài đêm đầu tiên. Nếu không thì nó cứ tiếp tục trì hoãn thôi.
Mei: Đồng ý. Bây giờ tôi thực sự hào hứng rồi. Cảm giác như cuối cùng chúng ta đã đưa ra được quyết định.
Dan: Chỉ mất ba tháng thôi! Nhưng đúng rồi — Việt Nam đó. Tối nay tôi sẽ bắt đầu tìm hiểu những địa điểm tốt nhất ở Hội An.

questions.prompt: Why have Mei and Dan not decided on a destination yet?
options: They have not had time to discuss it
They always come up with too many options
They cannot agree on a budget
They are waiting for cheaper flights
questions.explanation: Dan says: 'The problem is we always come up with too many options.'
questions.hint: Dan identifies the reason they have been going in circles.

questions.prompt: Why does Mei decide against Japan for this trip?
options: The return flights from Sydney are too expensive
The best season for Japan is not in summer
questions.explanation: Dan says return flights from Sydney 'are sitting around two thousand dollars each right now', which Mei describes as 'a stretch this year.'
questions.hint: Cost is the main barrier mentioned.

questions.prompt: What does Dan say he has heard about Hoi An?
options: It is famous for its nightlife
The food, old town, and cycling are incredible
It is one of the most affordable cities in Vietnam
questions.explanation: Dan says: 'I've heard incredible things about Hoi An — the food, the old town, the cycling.'
questions.hint: He lists three specific things he has heard about.

questions.prompt: What does Mei say she wants to include in the trip?
options: At least a few days in a quieter, coastal area
Time to visit local markets every morning
questions.explanation: Mei says: 'I'd want to include at least a few days somewhere quieter, maybe a coastal area.'
questions.hint: She says she doesn't want the whole trip to be city sightseeing.

questions.prompt: What itinerary does Dan suggest to give the trip 'a natural rhythm'?
options: Fly into Hanoi, take a train south, and end in Ho Chi Minh City
Spend the whole two weeks in Hoi An
Culture and food in Hanoi and Hoi An, then beach time in Da Nang or Phu Quoc
Start at the beach and then move to the cities
questions.explanation: Dan suggests: 'Hanoi and Hoi An for culture and food, then head down to Da Nang or Phu Quoc for some beach time.'
questions.hint: He describes it as giving the trip a natural structure.

questions.prompt: How much annual leave does Mei have available?
questions.explanation: Mei says: 'I've got two weeks left, so that's fine.'
questions.hint: She confirms her availability after Dan suggests a duration.

questions.prompt: What is Mei's preference for accommodation and spending?
options: Luxury hotels and fine dining restaurants
Camping to save as much money as possible
Mid-range guesthouses and spending more on experiences
Budget hostels so she can buy expensive souvenirs
questions.explanation: Mei says: 'I'd rather stay in mid-range guesthouses and spend more on experiences — good food, day trips, that kind of thing.'
questions.hint: She distinguishes between where to save and where to spend.

questions.prompt: What does Dan say about accommodation preferences?
options: He wants to stay in well-known international hotel chains
He is not bothered about luxury as long as the place is clean and comfortable
He prefers to rent private apartments rather than hotels
He wants to stay somewhere with a swimming pool
questions.explanation: Dan says: 'I'm not bothered about staying somewhere fancy. As long as the place is clean and comfortable, I'm happy.'

questions.prompt: How do they divide the planning tasks?
options: Dan books everything while Mei handles the packing
Mei looks at flights; Dan checks accommodation
They hire a travel agent to do everything
questions.explanation: Mei says: 'I can start looking at flights this weekend if you want to check the accommodation side.'
questions.hint: They agree on a specific split of responsibilities.

questions.prompt: By when do they agree to have flights and first nights booked?
options: By the end of next week
Before the end of the month
questions.explanation: Dan says: 'Let's set a deadline — say, end of next week — to have the flights and first few nights booked.'

questions.prompt: How long did it take them to make this decision, according to Dan?
questions.explanation: Dan jokes at the end: 'Only took us three months!'
questions.hint: Dan says this humorously in his final line.

questions.prompt: What does Dan plan to do after the conversation ends?
options: Start reading up on the best spots in Hoi An
Look up exchange rates for Vietnamese dong
questions.explanation: Dan says: 'I'll start reading up on the best spots in Hoi An tonight.'
questions.hint: He announces a specific action he will take that evening.

cloze.template:
Mei: Right, so we finally need to decide where we're going this summer. I feel like we've been talking about it for months without actually {{b1}} on anything.
Dan: I know, I know. The problem is we always come up with too many options. What are you actually in the mood for this time?
Mei: Honestly, something a bit different. I'd love to go somewhere neither of us has been before. Japan's been on my list for ages, but I know the flights aren't cheap.
Dan: Japan would be amazing, but I checked last week and return flights from Sydney are {{b2}} around two thousand dollars each right now. That's before hotels and food.
Mei: Yeah, that might be a bit of a {{b3}} this year. What about somewhere in Southeast Asia? Bali or Vietnam? The flights are much shorter and the budget goes a lot further.
Dan: Vietnam actually sounds really {{b4}} to me. I've heard incredible things about Hoi An — the food, the old town, the cycling. It seems like the sort of place you could spend a week and still not see everything.
Mei: I like that idea. Though I'll be honest — I'd want to include at least a few days somewhere quieter, maybe a coastal area. I don't want the whole trip to be city {{b5}}.
Dan: That's totally fair. We could do Hanoi and Hoi An for culture and food, then head down to Da Nang or Phu Quoc for some beach time. It gives the trip a natural {{b6}}.
Mei: Oh, that actually sounds like a really good structure. How long were you thinking? Two weeks?
Dan: I was going to suggest ten days to two weeks. I don't want to rush it, but I also don't want to blow through all my {{b7}} leave in one go. What have you got available?
Mei: I've got two weeks left, so that's fine. The tricky bit for me is keeping the overall budget manageable. I'd rather stay in {{b8}} guesthouses and spend more on experiences — good food, day trips, that kind of thing.
Dan: Completely agree. I'm not bothered about staying somewhere fancy. As long as the place is clean and comfortable, I'm happy. In Southeast Asia you can get really good value without spending a fortune.
Mei: Exactly. So shall we say Vietnam, roughly two weeks, mix of cities and coast? I can start looking at flights this weekend if you want to check the accommodation side.
Dan: Perfect. Let's {{b9}} a deadline — say, end of next week — to have the flights and first few nights booked. Otherwise it'll just keep drifting.
Mei: Agreed. I'm actually really excited now. It feels like we've finally made a decision.
Dan: Only took us three months! But yes — Vietnam it is. I'll start {{b10}} on the best spots in Hoi An tonight.

blanks.explanation: 'Settling on' is the phrasal verb meaning to finally choose or agree on something after considering options.
'Sitting around' in this context means prices are currently at approximately that level — a colloquial way to describe current market prices.
'A stretch' means something that is beyond what is comfortably achievable — used informally to say something is too expensive or difficult this time.
'Appealing' is the adjective used — it means attractive or attractive in a way that makes you want to do something.
'City sightseeing' refers to visiting tourist attractions in a city — Mei wants to balance this with quieter, more restful time.
'Natural rhythm' is the expression Dan uses — it suggests the trip has a pleasing, organic progression from one type of experience to another.
'Annual leave' is the standard term for a fixed number of days off work per year — Dan doesn't want to use it all at once.
'Mid-range' describes accommodation that is neither luxury nor basic — comfortable and reasonably priced.
'Set a deadline' is the standard collocation — 'set' is the verb that collocates naturally with 'deadline' in English.
'Reading up on' means to research a topic thoroughly by reading — it implies preparation and thorough study before an event.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Mei and Dan navigate differing preferences — she wants a quieter, coastal experience while he leans toward cultural exploration — but manage to find a compromise. In your experience, what makes travelling with another person rewarding or challenging? What strategies help people with different travel styles enjoy a trip together?
